title: Dynamic mechanical behavior of multilayer graphene via supersonic projectile penetration
abstract: Multilayer graphene is an exceptional anisotropic material due to its layered structure composed of two-dimensional carbon lattices. Although the intrinsic mechanical properties of graphene have been investigated at quasi-static conditions, its behavior under extreme dynamic conditions has not yet been studied. We report the high-strain-rate behavior of multilayer graphene over a range of thicknesses from 10 to 100 nanometers by using miniaturized ballistic tests. Tensile stretching of the membrane into a cone shape is followed by initiation of radial cracks that approximately follow crystallographic directions and extend outward well beyond the impact area. The specific penetration energy for multilayer graphene is ~10 times more than literature values for macroscopic steel sheets at 600 meters per second.
results: Many independent penetration experiments (47 events for  $v_i = 600 	ext{ m s}^{-1}$  and 43 events for  $v_i = 900 	ext{ m s}^{-1}$) were carried out for statistical analysis (figs. S4 and S5). The average apex angles ( $	heta_A$  in Fig. 1D) of petals for the 600– and 900–m s<sup>-1</sup> projectile velocities are 83  $	ext{±}$  21° (for 107 cracks) and 70  $	ext{±}$  23° (for 93 cracks), respectively, indicating that the higher tangential stresses induced at 900 m s<sup>-1</sup> were relaxed through more radial cracks.
discussion: Our microscopic ballistic results reveal that the superior in-plane speed of sound, high strength, stiffness, and structural anisotropy make MLG an extraordinary armor material exhibiting excellent impact energy delocalization under a supersonic penetration event. Because material far beyond the strike face area can also consume kinetic energy from the  $$ -bullet while the MLG membrane sustains high dynamic tensile stress, the μ-bullet effectively experiences a higher areal density material. As large-scale production of graphene-based composite materials is becoming possible, other graphene-like materials are being studied, and the results suggest opportunities for the use of ordered anisotropic nanocomposites for surprising mechanical applications.
conclusion: The good correspondence between the micro- and macroscopic projectile penetration tests, especially in the measured specific energy absorption, suggests that the microballistic method with its high-energy resolution may offer an effective means for the exploration of high-strain-rate physics of various materials, as well as practical advantages in rapid, high-throughput testing.
